data.title: Rates Spark: Positive Impulses, But Treasuries Are Not Moving
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

Wednesday probably sees daylight on the Supreme Court's decision on the IEEPA tariffs. It could rule them legit, and if so, we just move on. We suspect they will be struck down, and we'll probably still just move on. This Treasury market is showing a remarkable capacity to just not care too much about stuff. It won't last, but it's what we've got for now.

The CPI report was net positive for bonds

The biggest number for the bond market on Tuesday was the CPI report. It was tolerably good, in the sense that a 0.2% month-on-month/2.6% year-on-year outcome for core inflation some six months on from the instigation of a large rump of tariffs is a success, as the tariff effect is quite contained in terms of impact on consumer prices.

The impact effect on the 10yr Treasury yield was impressive, as it snapped lower. But it recovered higher subsequently. The resilience of the 10yr yield to various crosswinds has been remarkable of late.

It's baulking at excuses to snap both lower and higher in yield. The auctions this week have been solid too, with 3's 10's and 30's all showing consistent negative tails and decent auction details. But still, minimal follow-through.

The US deficit data so far has also been, if anything, a net positive

The US fiscal deficit for the first quarter of fiscal year 2026 came in at $601 billion, which is actually $110 billion less than the same time last year. A big reason is that tax revenues jumped - up 13%, or $141 billion - thanks to higher income and payroll taxes and a bump in customs duties after tariff changes.

Spending did go up a bit (2%, or $31 billion), but not enough to offset the revenue surge. There’s also a small wrinkle: some payments normally made on 1 January got pushed into December, but even adjusting for that, the deficit would still be about $112 billion lower than last year. Overall, revenues climbed faster than spending, giving the early year deficit a noticeable dip.

The angst with respect to the fiscal deficit has absolutely ramped down in recent months. The spread from 10yr SOFR to 10yr Treasuries is now in the 35bp area, following a steady trek down from the 55bp area seen in September 2025.

That journey has coincided with gradual improvements in the fiscal deficit position, helped by tariff revenue. It's having a meaningful effect, as had the spread remained at 55bp, the 10yr Treasury yield today would be in the area of 4.4% (rather than the sub-4.2% we see currently).

Supreme Court decision should really be impactful, but it probably won't be

Wednesday may well see the Supreme Court decision on the legality of the IEEPA tariffs. Our basic assumption is for them to be struck down, and we think the market is generally of the same opinion.

Confirmation of that would cause some consternation in the Trump administration, but we'd assume that a way will be found to re-erect the tariffs subsequently, either employing current acts of Congress, or writing new acts.

There is no plan B to the tariff plan of President Trump, which he reiterated at Tuesday's speech in Detroit. We're not expecting a huge market reaction as a result, but it will be messy should there be an avalanche of demands for tariff refunds.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: IJS: Choose AVUV Instead For Small-Cap Value Exposure
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Passive small-cap strategies like the one employed by the iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Value ETF (IJS) do not sufficiently screen for quality, thereby placing investor capital at risk when market conditions deteriorate. Instead, the actively managed Avantis US Small Cap Value ETF (AVUV) consistently produces a fundamentally superior portfolio, which is why it has outperformed IJS in every year since its inception. Given this alternative, I rate IJS a "sell."

Fund Overview

According to its fund page, IJT tracks the S&P SmallCap 600 Value Index, which "seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of small-capitalization U.S. equities that exhibit value characteristics." These value characteristics include a stock's book value-to-price ratio, its earnings-to-price ratio, and its sales-to-price ratio. However, the Index also indirectly holds stocks exhibiting weak growth and momentum characteristics, since all S&P SmallCap 600 Index stocks are scored separately on both the value and growth dimensions.

IJS's fund characteristics include a 0.18% expense ratio, a 0.03% 30-day median bid/ask spread, $7.4 billion in net assets, and a 1.53% trailing distribution yield paid quarterly.

IJS Sector Allocations vs. AVUV

As of January 10, 2026, IJS's two largest sectors were Financials (21%) and Consumer Discretionary (17%). However, relative to AVUV, these sectors are 4.5% and 2.10% underweight, respectively. Per the comparison below, IJS's largest overweights are in Real Estate and Health Care. Notably, its exposure to REITs indicates that not all distributions received are qualified.

IJS Performance Analysis

The following log-scaled chart highlights how AVUV's performance lead over IJS has steadily increased since October 2019, the first full month after AVUV's inception. This trend is relevant because it shows that AVUV can outperform in both bull and bear markets, including by 6.43% in 2022 and 8.14% in 2023.

Crucial to AVUV's relative success was its ability to recover from its 16.41% decline from April 2022 to September. As shown below, AVUV recovered by November 2022, whereas IJS experienced both a deeper drawdown (20.65%) and a much longer recovery time. Overall, IJS was underwater by nearly two more years (23 months) during this bear market.

The statistics I compiled from 26 small-cap value ETFs indicate that capital efficiency ratios (ROA, ROE, and ROTC) are closely related to an ETF's recovery time among small-cap funds. Specifically, I found a moderate negative correlation between recovery time and return on total capital for drawdowns ending in September 2022. I also found that Russell 2000 Value Index funds like the iShares Russell 2000 Value ETF (IWN) and the Vanguard Russell 2000 Value Index Fund ETF (VTWV), which have the lowest ROTC figures, have yet to recover as of December 2025. Therefore, it's reasonable to make quality a key metric when evaluating the resiliency of a small-cap value ETF.

IJS Primary Weakness: Poor Quality

IJS's components, as part of the S&P 1500 Composite Index, must meet a positive profitability requirement upon entry. That gives it a slight edge over the Russell 2000 Value ETFs, but overall, IJS's quality is still below average for small-cap value funds. Based on its current composition, IJS ranks #31/40 on ROA, #33/40 on ROE, and #31/40 on ROTC compared to #19/40, #20/40, and #18/40 rankings for AVUV. Therefore, it's evident that AVUV's managers intentionally make capital efficiency a key part of the selection process. I've highlighted these three statistics, plus several margin metrics for IJS's top ten holdings, in the table below.

For small-cap stocks, where margins are generally below those of large-caps, profitability levels are just as important as the direction. This matters because companies operating close to an acceptable margin have increased drawdown risk when market conditions deteriorate. Subsequently, weak capital efficiency ratios limit their ability to reinvest when conditions improve, leading to slower recovery times. This partially explains why IJS performed reasonably well relative to AVUV in 2024 and 2025. During these years, positive market conditions supported lower-margin businesses, but over the full cycle, AVUV weathered uncertainty much better.

IJS Secondary Weakness: Weak Growth and Valuation Mix

AVUV also has a meaningful growth and valuation advantage over IJS. As highlighted below, AVUV's current components increased their earnings per share by an annualized 0.72% over the last three years, while IJS's have declined by 6.04% over the same period. AVUV also has the advantage on three-year sales CAGR (4.26% vs. 1.14%), one-year estimated sales growth (4.09% vs. 3.65%), and one-year estimated EPS growth (5.03% vs. 2.80%).

The table also shows IJS currently trades at 15.57x trailing earnings, which is two multiple points (15%) more than AVUV. Given IJS's inferior quality and growth exposure, this shouldn't happen from a factor investing perspective, and its mix increases the chances of IJS trailing AVUV as markets rotate their preference between each factor.

Risks To Thesis

My thesis could be invalidated if factor discrimination reduces over the next cycle. This scenario would mean that AVUV's higher-quality, higher-growth approach to small-cap value becomes less important, and IJS might benefit more than AVUV because its top ten sub-industry concentration levels indicate greater diversification - 33.8% vs. 43.6%, per the table below.

However, I don't believe this scenario is likely. To illustrate why, consider the following calendar-year returns for various SmallCap 600 Index ETFs covering the value (IJS), growth (IJT), momentum (XSMO), and low-volatility (XSLV) from 2016 to 2025. Cross-factor differences, defined as the variance between the best- and worst-performing factors, averaged 15.82% over this period.

This data indicates that opportunities exist for fund managers aiming to optimize small-cap indices. AVUV does not have a significant advantage over IJS on the value factor, as the two have similar valuation ratios covering sales, cash flow, earnings, and book value. However, its managers have designed a portfolio that's at least competitive on valuation, while significantly enhancing exposure to other key factors, including quality and growth.

Investment Recommendation

With $7.4 billion in assets under management and a 25+ year track record, IJS is a popular choice for investors seeking broad small-cap value exposure. However, it's not an ideal choice. AVUV offers investors similar exposure to the size and value factors while strengthening its overall factor mix, particularly on quality and growth. As a result, it's the superior choice, and I rate IJS a "sell."

data.title: Benign U.S. Inflation Keeps The Door Open For At Least Two Further Rate Cuts
data.description:
By James Knightley, Chief International Economist, US

Inflation undershoots expectations

The December CPI report has provided another soft inflation print, with headline prices rising 0.3% month-on-month as the consensus forecast, while core inflation rose only 0.2% versus the 0.3% consensus prediction.

We thought there was a strong chance of an even higher print of 0.4%, in large part due to timing issues related to the government shutdown that heightened the influence of price discounting later in November 2025 relative to a 'normal' November 2024. We thought this would have led to a bigger seasonally adjusted bounce for December price levels.

The key takeaway is that goods prices were very benign, which underscores the point that tariffs have had a far more muted impact on inflation than feared.

Goods that are often imported and therefore subject to tariffs include appliances (-4.3% MoM), furniture (-0.4% MoM), new vehicles (0.0% MoM) and video and audio equipment (-0.4% MoM). This is a remarkable story and suggests US retailers are squeezing their profit margins. Used vehicles also fell in price (-1.1%).

Core goods prices show limited tariff impact

Door remains wide open for two interest rate cuts

In terms of sectors that continue to run hotter, the key primary rent and owners' equivalent rent increased 0.3%, medical care services rose 0.4% and transport services increased 0.5%, largely due to a 5.2% jump in airline fares. Apparel prices increased 0.6% and food prices increased 0.7%.

This is a very good outcome overall, and with Fed Chair Jerome Powell suggesting that the tariff impact hasn't got much further to run and could peak in this current quarter, it indicates that policymakers are on course to cut interest rates further.

We still see a bit of risk from tariffs, but the fact it has come through so slowly and much less forcibly than feared gives the opportunity for falling gasoline prices, slowing housing rents and weakening wage growth to contribute to CPI continuing to trend lower this year, getting very close to 2% towards the end of the year. As such, two Fed rate cuts seem perfectly achievable, with the risks skewed towards a third due to the cooling jobs story.

Jobs and affordability will be key for the mid-terms

With regard to the jobs numbers, the ADP private payrolls data suggest employment increased 11,750 per week on average in the four weeks to 20 December. That's consistent with the 50k monthly increase in non-farm payrolls, which is what the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) reported last week for December.

Remember, though, that Powell indicated that the Fed believes payrolls growth is being overestimated by around 60,000 per month, so effectively this suggests the labour market is effectively stagnant.

Household affordability concerns are set to be the key issue at the mid-term elections in November. Easing price pressures are a positive development, but it is clear from consumer confidence surveys that households are not necessarily believing it just yet.

We suspect the bigger driver of anxiety is the cooling jobs market, where all sectors outside of government, leisure and hospitality and private education and healthcare services have lost jobs for seven out of the past eight months.

BLS calculations for % of economic output accrued to workers in the form of compensation

On top of this, the chart above shows the BLS’s calculations for the proportion of economic growth that is accrued to workers via their pay, which has dropped from 64% in the 1950s to less than 54% today.

With the corporate sector receiving an increasing share of the benefits of economic growth, this goes a long way to explain why consumer confidence is so weak relative to robust GDP prints and record-high equity markets.

This suggests we could see more efforts from the Administration to generate positive headlines, including the proposed capping of credit card borrowing costs, measures to improve mortgage availability and affordability and removing some items from the scope of tariffs. It also underscores the importance to the President of being able to deliver on the proposed $2,000 tariff dividend ahead of the mid-term elections.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Palladyne: Lost Ground, But Mid-Tier Prime Positioning May Drive Interest Again
data.description:
Background

Last year, we had initiated coverage on Palladyne AI Corp (PDYN), whose proprietary AI/ML foundational technology has the potential to enhance the capabilities of industrial and defense robots, and make them more efficient and autonomous in real time (PDYN management likes to refer to their business as a facilitator of “embodied AI” where systems which leverage this tech, don’t just analyze data, but also sense, decide and act in real time).

To better understand the long-term opportunity at stake and the underlying prowess of PDYN’s IP (relative to the competition), investors may consider perusing our June 2025 article on the stock, for which we have a hold rating. As you can see from the image below, the PDYN stock has since experienced quite a volatile journey and is down by over 22%, underperforming other robotic stocks (which are up by nearly 20% on average), and the extended market (which is up by 13%).

Nonetheless, since it’s been over six months since we last reviewed PDYN, we thought it would be a good time to gauge the temperature in this counter and see what’s putting a lid on the stock from flourishing.

Key Financial Metrics Not in Best Shape

For starters, one could argue that the market is finding it difficult to warm up to a company whose revenue profile isn’t progressing higher on a YoY basis. Forget about progressing, for the first nine months of 2025, total revenue of $3.5M was roughly only half as much as what was seen a year ago!

Also consider that the positive bottom line reading of $11.5M was largely only due to a non-cash accounting item of $33.1M (which was due to a positive fair value reading of its warrants, something that is bound to fluctuate from quarter to quarter)

The glass-half-full brethren may well point to total OPEX drifting lower by around 3% YoY to $26.7M, but this component still includes a lot of non-cash items, and rather one should focus more on the cadence of operating cash outflows, that are trending higher by 9% YoY at over $19.1M. Going forward, management is also on record stating that the quantum of sales stuff will step up over the next 6-12 months.

While all this can be quite concerning on the surface, we don’t think investors should get overly fixated over the revenue aspect, as part of the revenue attrition is driven by PDYN transitioning its revenue profile away from legacy hardware sales (as part of their previous avatar they used to be in the business of products like exoskeletons) which are being willingly phased out by the management. To provide further clarity, note that back in 2024, PDYN’s revenue profile consisted of both research and development contract agreements (62% of sales), as well as hardware and repair services sales (38% of sales). However, one year on, and the revenue mix is pretty much oriented towards product development contract sales.

Yes, one could then argue why product development contract sales haven’t also been resilient enough (it is down by -18% YoY for 9M-25 at $3.58M). Well, here, timing tends to play a key role, and can often drive lumpiness across quarters; more so, PDYN is also increasingly garnering contracts from US government agencies, and the shutdown last year not only impacted collection efforts from the government, but also prevented them from interacting and consummating new contracts.

We would also note that previously while PDYN management was reluctant to put concrete numbers on the sales cycle of their industrial and defense-industrial product Palladyne IQ (of which customer testing only began in June 2024), the most recent 10Q now suggested that the sales cycle is relatively long at 12-18 months, based on conversations with dozens of customers. For their second product- Palladyne Pilot, whose applications extend to UAV missions, and was made commercially available in Q1-25, the sales cycle is still yet to be estimated.

Note however, that in October 2025, PDYN got into a collaborative agreement with Draganfly Inc. (DPRO), the latter of which will now leverage the Palladyne Pilot AI software to facilitate more expanded mission capabilities and superior operator efficiency in their efforts to service their government, defense, and commercial clients.

New Defense-Tech Avatar Appears To Be Paying Off

Against this backdrop, a few days after its Q3 results in mid-November, PDYN also announced a strategic shift in their positioning, with the intention of transitioning from a software innovator into a vertically-integrated defense tech specialist, with strengths in three key areas- advanced avionics, component manufacturing, and their much bandied-embodied AI IP. All these capabilities will be harnessed at Palladyne Defense, a new unit that has now been created. Do consider that PDYN hasn’t just built these capabilities overnight and rather had to engage in a few acquisitions (that of GuideTech LLC, an aerospace and avionics design and engineering specialist, and two Crucis manufacturing companies that currently already support key defense programs).

So, what’s the underlying motivation behind this strategic pivot you ask?

Well, investors should consider that there’s great appetite by the Department of Defense [DoD] to reshore defense production, and PDYN hopes to exploit this wave by addressing gaps in the “mid-tier” prime segment. While the large primes have the infrastructure and scaling capabilities, they are not seen as dexterous enough to drive innovation and shift with the times. At the other end of the equation, small startups are bursting with ideas and interesting IP, but lack the ability to scale and effectively service government contracts. PDYN believes they can combine the best of both worlds as they attempt to make a mark on the defense-tech market.

Initially the market didn’t seem too chuffed about this development (one could maybe argue that PDYN is suffering from an identity crisis, as it used to be involved in hardware during its Sarcos Robotics days, but then felt it was too capital intensive and pivoted to software in 2024, and now once again they are including hardware manufacturing through the recent acquisitions), but the shifting financial expectations suggest that that something good is coming off it.

To elaborate, back in November, PDYN suggested that their FY26 revenue in 2026 would triple from the $7.8M figure seen in FY24 (which would have implied a figure of around $24M), while the 18-month backlog would be over $10M. Now, as of their 13 th January press release, FY26 revenue is expected to come in at a higher range of $24M-$27M, while the backlog has already stepped up to $13M, with the majority of this expected to be recognized over the next 12 months, reflecting the dexterity of this new mid prime avatar. Investors should also consider that even though PDYN as a whole will face a step-up in OPEX in 2026, the acquired businesses, will still generate positive EBITDA this year.

How is consensus positioned now?

Well firstly note that consensus is only budgeting for a figure of$4.39M for FY25, as they didn’t expect the acquisitions to contribute 6 weeks of revenue in Q4. PDYN believes those 6 weeks of acquired revenue could see the FY25 revenue come in 20% higher than expectations at $5.25M (mid-point of guidance). The FY26 revenue boost won’t necessarily be a humungous surprise as consensus was already guiding to a figure of close to $25M (the mid-point of management’s guidance would point to a figure of $25.5M)

Closing Thoughts- What Do The Technicals Look Like?

While we wait for all this to play out, investors should consider that bearish hues around PDYN have stepped up since we last covered it. Relative to where it was at the start of H2-25, the total shares sold short has risen by over two-thirds and currently stands at 6.758 shares. As a function of the total float, it now represents an elevated threshold of over 20%; this percentage could potentially have been a lot higher, but as we’ve noted previously, PDYN was always going to raise funds through equity and end up boosting the share count. Besides, the acquisition of Greentech and the two Crucis companies in November 2025, also prompted another spike in the share count as 65% of the agggregate$31M purchase price was paid in stock.

It's also fair to say that the bulls don’t quite have the same conviction on PDYN as before (although there’s potential for this to get restimulated again given the recent news flow).

The last time we covered PDYN, it was trading above all its key moving averages (50, 100, and 200) on the daily charts. Now that is no longer the case, with the drop below the 200DMA (blue line) in particular proving to be quite troublesome considering how long it had defended that. Meanwhile also consider that since October, the stock has been showcasing a series of lower-lows and lower-highs, although for now, there appears to be signs of an intermediate bottom at the sub $4.2 levels. The recent news flow pointing to a higher revenue guide and backlog for 2026, will likely also ensure that PDYN doesn’t retest those levels any time soon.

data.title: Trump's Credit Card Cap: Who Really Loses Access To Credit
data.description:
The facts

On Friday, January 9, after the stock market closed, President Donald Trump posted an update on social media platform Truth Social announcing his intentions to introduce a 10% cap on credit card interest rates and apply them for only one year. There was no formal executive order, nor was there a draft of proposed legislation. The message was a political statement, but it generated panic and specifically within the banking/credit-card industries.

It's worth noting to those who have not been tracking the current news flow that this announcement will only become active if it is approved through Congress as a law.

That being said, we know one thing about how the President operates that should not be ignored. Mr. Trump has often employed a particular type of negotiation strategy in the past: aim high at the start, dominate the media coverage, and then begin to move toward compromise (as evidenced by the tariff announcements) or simply delay implementation of the measure. In many instances, Mr. Trump did not intend to implement the announced measure, but rather the announcement served as a negotiating tactic to achieve a different objective or to send a political signal.

Maybe I am completely wrong; however, even with this being stated, the 10 percent level may just be the beginning, and possibly the final destination will be an even less restricted form of action. It could simply be informal pressure on the industry, or nothing concrete, some concessions on fees, business practices, or consumer protection. Anything is possible, but I believe he's also doing it to garner more votes, given the upcoming midterm elections. We will only know for sure after a couple of weeks when the media dust settles.

As stated, the marketplace did not wait for clarity from Washington. The banking industry and the large banks, which are responsible for providing the majority of revolving credit in the United States responded as though regulatory risk was now already in effect as law. This is exactly how it works in financial markets; once the mere thought of a price ceiling is introduced into the political dialogue, investors begin to ask questions.

The questions include two main ones:

  1. What would happen to a system that operates at an average rate of 22-23 percent, and then someone attempts to regulate those rates down by half for a one year period?
  2. When the price of credit is capped, does not automatically guarantee that credit will continue to be available.

A price cap not only limits the cost of the loan; it also limits the amount of loans that are available. The history of credit shows how intermediaries react to price caps by limiting their volume of business. Fewer cards issued, lower limits on individual card purchases, stricter customer qualification requirements. This is an established phenomenon with similarities to monetary tightening through the Fed raising interest rates. Banks can voluntarily act in this matter: freeze rate increases to all new accounts, decrease APRs to certain types of consumers, provide flexibility to account holders who are having difficulty making payments (payment deferments, installment plans, temporary changes to interest rates). This is not the first time banks have done so and as always, it would be the customary segment of the population that pays the price.

Wall Street Reaction

Investors responded quickly to the decision. I still remember talking with some colleagues, and I discussed at length the potential for Monday's opening and which companies might be affected most by the announcement - and clearly, the market viewed the announcement as a regulatory risk and possible credit contraction.

Companies with the greatest decline were those who rely most heavily on revolving credit; Capital One (COF) is the worst of the bunch (-9.48%), followed closely by Affirm (AFRM -6.91%) and American Express (AXP -6.51%). Networks such as Visa (V -6.85%) and Mastercard (MA -6.08%) fell nearly as hard as did AXP - although they do not make money off of interest. Here again, the second-order impact is what investors priced-in - fewer lines of credit = fewer opportunities to spend. Diversified large bank (Citi -3.56%, U.S. Bank -2.67%) performed better than their smaller bank counterparts, not because they have less exposure to the negative impacts, but because they have greater flexibility to absorb the potential costs.

Even if the law doesn't pass, the political framework has already insinuated itself into investors' minds, and when it happens, multiples fall first and explanations come later. Investors reacted to the decision so quickly because investors understand how a price cap works -- if a price cap is established without impacting losses related to credit, funding, or operating costs, the most logical response is not to provide more credit, but rather to limit the amount of credit extended. That is precisely what banking associations and management (such as JPM) are already saying.

Our belief is that actions like this will have the exact opposite consequence to what the administration wants for consumers. Instead of lowering the price of credit, we’ll simply reduce the supply of credit, and that will be bad for everyone: consumers, the wider economy, and yes, at the margin, for us. Barnum said.

How Credit Cards Can Cost 22-23% Today

Many students learn about this topic as freshmen, in the first years, but it may help many people understand the basis for a seemingly unfair practice. The following chart makes it clear that the average APR on credit cards was steady for nearly 10 years (post financial crisis until 2019) at 13% – 16%, then something changed. Since 2021, the APRs started climbing dramatically and now exceed 22% in 2024 – 2025.

At least four elements contribute to the APR of a credit card; no one of which can be eliminated.

  1. One element is the cost of capital. Banks do not loan money for nothing. With Fed Funds rates rising significantly above pre-COVID rates, along with the cost of funding continuing to remain high, the structural rate floor has increased. Even if the Fed cuts rates immediately, we would not magically go back to the 2016 world.
  2. A second factor is credit loss. Credit cards are unsecured credit. As the economic cycle turns, there will be more defaults, particularly in the subprime segments. Therefore, the credit loss risk must be accounted for before the damage occurs and not after the damage is done.
  3. The third factor is operational expense; i.e., technology, anti-fraud, regulatory compliance, servicing, etc. All of these expenses increase as the complexity of the system increases and do not decrease simply because a politician has decided otherwise.
  4. The fourth factor is the cost of reward/loyalty programs; i.e., cashback, points, miles, etc. These rewards are not free. They are mostly financed by the interest paid by those who continue to rotate their balances each month.

Together, these factors demonstrate why the average APR has continued to climb. While I could be incorrect, the basic fact remains that the credit card market is a fine-tuned equilibrium between price, risk and accessibility.

I thought the exercise that Goldman Sachs created was a relatively simple way to think about how a credit card works. Below, I will provide the exercise with my own description of how it works.

Assuming a $100 mn portfolio of card loans and a 10% possible profit rate, at which point you subtract the cost of capital (approximately 3%) and operational expense (approximately 7%) from the profit rate plus credit losses (over 4%). The final result is a pre-tax loss. Therefore, setting a 10% cap does not "reduce profitability." Rather, it causes a significant amount of revolving credit to be unprofitable. And, once a product has become structurally loss-making the question is not what it costs. The question is, "will it continue to exist?" This appears fairly obvious so far. The next logical step from there becomes somewhat automatic. In other words, if the price cannot reflect the risk then the adjustment will not be reflected by the price. The adjustment will be made by adjusting the quantity. There will be fewer cards, lower spending limits, and/or tighter criteria for approving cards. And, it is exactly at this juncture, we have an intersection to the next subject: who gets to stay in and who gets to stay out.

Real Impact

It is quite obvious that if a credit card's expected return drops below a certain level, the bank will stop lending. The bank will stop opening new accounts, decrease limits on existing accounts, and make tighter requirements for approval of all applicants. It is another form of Credit Rationing similar to a Monetary Taper, but even more targeted.

Morgan Stanley (see chart above) notes that a larger percentage of people in the middle- and low-income brackets and those with lower credit scores have Revolving Debt. In addition, they are more likely to be "Maxed Out" which means they have reached or exceeded their limit. To these individuals, the Card is not just a luxury item, but rather some sort of cash flow cushion. Therefore, if the bank reduces or closes the line of credit to these individuals, there will be a seriously problem. I am talking about access to capital or no access at all. When access to credit is removed, there is a shift in spending patterns. At first, spending declines on discretionary items. Then, as the process continues, it begins to decline on more essential items. I personally believe that a Rate Cap appears to be a Pro-Consumer measure, however, it has the potential to create the opposite outcome. Those who have stronger financial resources remain within the system and possibly pay less in interest. Those with weaker financial resources are pushed outside of the system. And that is when the issues cease to be simply financial, and begin to be structural.

K-shaped economy

The U.S. economy has increasingly moved in a K-shaped pattern, where

  • the upper portion of society has seen steady increases in their income, and the value of their assets (e.g., homes) has increased, and they have had easy access to credit;
  • whereas the lower portion of society has experienced an increase in costs of goods and services, stagnant wages that cannot keep pace with increasing prices, and are using revolving credit as a means to make ends meet.

The rate cap, in isolation, will only assist individuals who remain within the existing financial systems. Individuals with a good credit rating, high credit limits, and steady income will continue to have access to credit cards and will ultimately be paying less interest on their cards. They'll see the average rate drop from 15% to 10%. Therefore, this will ultimately be a positive change for these individuals.

Individuals who are more vulnerable and have poor credit ratings, are nearing the maximum amount allowed on their current cards, or have poor credit history may find themselves locked out from access to new cards.

I might be wrong, but if the policy goal was to help the most pressured consumers, this is a very inefficient way to do it.

Winners & Losers

Moving back to the markets. From a company standpoint, as I stated above, the market reaction has generally been consistent. It hasn't fallen into the "sector" equally; however, it has provided a pretty good indication of which business models are fragile and can withstand political noise, and which ones cannot.

Those most impacted have been card issuers dependent upon revolving credit, and those who have built their business model around store cards (Capital One, Synchrony, for example). Both groups rely upon a relatively basic equation: High APRs, used to offset both credit loss and operating expense/funding. Therefore it is no surprise that these companies' stock prices were viewed in a "binary" light: Either there will be no cap, or earnings will be significantly reduced. Even if one assumes that Mr. Trump is going to aim high and then retreat to lower ground (a now familiar pattern, such as what happened with the "TACO strategy") the asymmetric risk still exists on the wrong side. While I can't get into every single company individually (the length of this analysis is already becoming excessive), avoiding this segment of the market, at least currently, may make more sense than trying to gauge when this segment of the market will recover. Discussing "buying the dip" in this area is more of a wager on the political outcome rather than an investment decision, and at current prices that is a risk not worth taking.

American Express (AXP) seems to fall into a middle category. While the company is subject to some interest rate exposure, it is not totally dependent upon that interest. Approximately 75% of their earnings come from other sources (such as commissions, premium consumer spending, annual membership fees, etc.) and other service areas. The​‍​‌‍​‍‌ market has been selling it almost as much as pure credit players. If the headline issue (the 10%) goes away, or is somehow mitigated, American Express could be one of the few businesses that would be able to take a breath and "be itself" again. The chart below also shows that American Express gets approximately 75% of its revenue from customer spending and services, and not from revolving credit alone.

Then Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA). They do not lend money; therefore, they do not collect interest. Visa and Mastercard earn revenue based on the volume of transactions processed. The fact that Visa and Mastercard have declined to nearly the same extent as American Express is primarily due to the fact that the markets are pricing in the regulatory risks associated with the White House's targeting of the payments industry. This is a second order type of risk. There will be fewer purchases made if there is less credit available to consumers; therefore, there will be fewer transaction volumes. However, this is an indirect risk, not a fundamental risk to the businesses of Visa and Mastercard.

What matters most to investors

Ultimately, it is not about whether the 10% rate-cap becomes law as currently proposed. Personally, I don't believe it will pass as-is, but again, that is not what ultimately matters. What does matter is that regulatory risk has now been priced into prices. When regulatory risk has been priced in, investors will no longer wait for regulators to make their decision.

In essence, not all credit card companies are created equally. Companies dependent upon the high APR revolving credit area will be more susceptible to instability than those that are not dependent. The risk that these companies experience is not just a volatile risk, it is a long-term risk, specifically a need for them to drastically change their model. In this case, downturns in the segment will not necessarily be a buying opportunity.

Companies such as American Express have been negatively affected by the "contagion effect" of this, but the company, for example, is also not completely at risk and can absorb the pressures associated with the imposition of higher rates without harming its shareholders. Fundamentally, the sell off we are experiencing now is essentially due to politics and not indicative of a short term decline in the fundamentals.

The stories surrounding Visa and Mastercard are different still. The risk to their stocks is different. The risk to Visa and Mastercard is second-order; that is, a potential decrease in volume and/or negative changes in consumer sentiment, and not a deterioration in their interest margin.

I may be incorrect but I believe that this entire process will not conclude with a 10% cap on rates. Instead, I think that it will conclude with some form of diluted version of the cap, a compromise, or simply nothing.

data.title: TPG: Jackson Financial Deal A Positive But Valuation Now Less Compelling (Ratings Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of TPG Inc. (TPG) have delivered a total return of 42% since my previous piece, TPG: Initiating A Strong Buy Following Solid Q1 Results, was published on May 27, 2025.

The move higher in TPG shares has been driven by solid business performance and earnings growth. I view the company's recently announced strategic partnership with Jackson Financial as a key long-term positive. While the stock's valuation remains reasonably attractive, it has become significantly less compelling relative to peers. For these reasons I continue to view the stock as attractive but am downgrading to Buy from Strong Buy.

Jackson Financial Strategic Partnership

On January 6, 2026 TPG announced that it has established a strategic partnership with Jackson Financial Inc. (JXN). As part of the agreement, TPG will manage a minimum of $12 billion in AUM for Jackson Financial with economic incentives aligned to a long-term target of $20 billion. Initially, TPG will be managing investment grade ABF and Direct Lending strategies for Jackson. As part of the deal TPG will invest $500 million in Jackson Financial representing a 6.5% pro forma ownership stake. Jackson Financial will receive $150 million of of TPG stock, priced at market, with potential to receive additional shares if the amount of assets managed by TPG for Jackson Financial grows to $20 billion.

I view the deal is highly attractive for TPG as it allows the company to strengthen its insurance asset management vertical in a relatively capital light way. Managing insurance assets are an attractive business for TPG given the long duration of insurance capital which provides predictable fees over the long-run. While the $500 million TPG investment in Jackson Financial is significant, TPG will be a minority shareholder and will not need to consolidate Jackson Financial's balance sheet with its own. As a result, I expect the market to continue valuing TPG as a capital light alternative asset manager (peers such as Blackstone, Ares, and Blue Owl) as opposed to more a capital intensive alternative asset manager, peers such as Apollo Global Management, Inc. (APO) and KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR).

The deal will boost TPG's insurance solutions business to $30 billion from $18 billion currently which represents significant growth. The deal is a non-exclusive partnership and thus I expect TPG to continue growing its insurance investment management business by adding other third party managers as clients. Managing a larger amount of insurance assets and having skin in the game in terms of ownership of Jackson Financial should position TPG to be a stronger player in this vertical unlocking future AUM growth from other insurance companies. Moreover, Jackson Financial has total AUM of $350 billion so over the long-run I would not be surprised to see the amount of AUM managed by TPG increase above the $20 billion long-term target initially announced.

The deal includes a minimum fee of 50bps throughout the life of the partnership and the transaction is expected to be accretive to TPG's fee related earnings per share by Q4 2026. Thus far, the deal has received a muted reaction as TPG shares have underperformed roughly inline with peers since the announcement. One reason why I believe this is the case that TPG shares have significantly outperformed peers of late and relative valuation has become somewhat less attractive. Additionally, TPG had flagged insurance as a growth vertical and was said to be considering other deals previously, such as Brighthouse Financial, Inc. (BHF). For this reason, market participants were not surprised to see the company announce a deal. Jackson Financial shares have benefited more and are up roughly 7% since the deal was announced.

Valuation Update

TPG shares currently trade at 22.3x consensus FY 2026 distributable earnings per share ("DE"), the metric which is widely used to value alternative asset managers given the volatile nature of non-cash items included in other metrics. Comparably, at the time of my prior piece the stock was trading at 23x consensus forward DE and thus from a multiple standpoint the stock is trading at a similar level as was previously the case. However, the near-term DE growth outlook has come down somewhat as TPG is expected to deliver DE growth of 26% for FY 2026 down from a prior growth estimate of 41%. Consensus estimates call for FY 2027 DE growth of 25% which is roughly inline with the growth estimate at the time of my previous piece.

At the time of my prior piece the S&P 500 had been trading at roughly 22x forward earnings and today the index continues to trade at the same forward multiple. Given TPG's significantly higher near-term growth prospects relative to the broader market I continue to view the stock as attractive.

In regards to peer valuation, TPG has become somewhat less attractive than was previously the case. At the time of my previous piece, TPG's peers shown below were trading at an average forward P/E ratio of 25.7x. Currently, the group trades at an average FY 2026 P/E ratio of 21.8x. Thus, the group has experienced significant multiple contraction while TPG has also experienced only very modest multiple contraction. Previously TPG shares had traded at a discount to both KKR and Blue Owl but that has now reversed and TPG is trading at a premium. I view the valuation premium relative to Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) and KKR is questionable given the fact that these companies have similar near-term growth prospects to TPG. Overall, TPG's valuation is still reasonable relative to peers but is not as compelling as was previously the case.

Private Credit Concerns

One thing which has recently weighted on TPG shares as well as the broader alternative asset manager group is concerns related to private credit. The First Brands and Tricolor bankruptcies have raised questions about the quality of private credit lending. Additionally, BDC's have come under selling pressure after Blue Owl's failed attempt to merge its non-traded BDC Blue Owl Capital Corp II with Blue Owl Capital Corp. The proposed transaction called into question the marks of private debt securities as the transaction would have seen holders of the non-traded BDC would have seen a 20% if the merger had been completed as planned.

TPG has moderate exposure to private credit as its TPG AG Credit business accounts for roughly 19% of management fees. Private credit makes up a significantly larger share of revenue for peers such as Blue Owl, Apollo, and Ares. While TPG has less near-term risk due to private credit concerns than peers, any slow down in the growth of private credit fundraising would still be a significant negative as private credit is expected to be a growth driver for the firm and make up a larger share of AUM and management fees in the future.

Ultimately, I do not believe the recent challenges experienced in the private credit market are systematic. Rather, I believe these situations represent one off events and that the private credit AUM growth story is likely to remain intact for the next few years. That said, I see some potential risk for softer fundraising over the next couple quarters as investors take a wait and see approach as the dust settles.

Macro Remains A Key Risk

In my previous piece I noted that a sustained economic downturn represents a key risk to the TPG bull case. That remains the case today as TPG continues to have significant exposure to higher risk / higher return investments. Compared to most peers, TPG continues to have more relative exposure to private equity which tends to be more cyclical relative to private credit and real estate. I continue to maintain a fairly positive near-term macroeconomic outlook and do not expect a recession in 2026. That said, the recent slowdown in job growth is a potential concern and something investors should closely monitor for signs of potential macroeconomic weakness. While TPG shares would likely underperform the broader market in the event of a sustained economic downturn, I believe the company's asset light business model and significant proportion of long-term locked up capital would allow the firm to manage through any macro related challenges.

Conclusion

TPG shares have delivered very strong results for investors since my initial Strong Buy piece was published. The stock's gains have been driven by strong business performance and earnings growth. I view TPG's recently announced strategic partnership with Jackson Financial as a key positive development.

I continue to view TPG shares valuation as attractive relative to the broader market, due to above market growth prospects, but less so than was previously the case due. Moreover, TPG shares have become less attractive on a relative basis compared to peers as the stock now trades roughly inline with its peer group. Comparably, the stock had been trading at a significant discount a the time of my previous piece. While I do not believe recent private credit challenges will prove to be systemic, I would not be surprised to see a slowdown in fundraising activity as investors wait for the dust to settle. For these reasons, I am downgrading TPG shares to Buy from Strong Buy.

data.title: Where Are We Currently Finding Value?
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Fiduciary Management Q4 2025 Investment Strategy Outlook.

Despite the challenging backdrop, we continue to find attractive investment opportunities. The following are a few stock examples where we are currently finding value:

Hayward Holdings Inc. (HAYW) – Small Cap

Hayward Holdings is a leading global pool equipment manufacturer, primarily serving the residential pool market. North America accounts for 85% of sales and over 90% of profits. Product categories include Pumps, Automation and Sanitization, Heaters, Filters, Lighting and Water Features, and Cleaners. The company estimates that 80% of total sales come from their existing installed base of pools (50% repair and replacement), making the business relatively resilient to economic cycles. Hayward also exhibits strong pricing power as equipment purchase decisions are typically made by pool service professionals, who are less sensitive to price than the end customer. Pool service professionals also tend to be risk averse and primarily purchase equipment from the leading players in the industry. These dynamics lead to high margins and ROIC. The pool industry went through a period of supercharged growth in the early parts of Covid, which gave way to a period of extremely weak end-market demand, exacerbated by destocking in the distribution channel. The industry has since stabilized but is still experiencing lackluster demand for newly built and remodeled pools. We believe that over time, the discretionary sides of the business should recover. In the meantime, Hayward should be able to continue raising prices and growing volumes within the installed base of pools. They have a solid balance sheet, strong management team, and reasonable valuation multiple, particularly given their depressed earnings.

Accenture Plc Ci A (ACN) – Large Cap/All Cap/Global

Accenture is the world's leading IT consultant, with advantages stemming from their depth and breadth across products, geographies, and industries. Their revenue is split roughly in half between IT consulting and managed services. Over the last four years, Accenture's valuation has roughly halved. They've faced headwinds in IT spending and suffered from the perception that they are an AI loser. We believe that AI will cause deflationary pressure in parts of their business, but that it will be more than offset by the work required for enterprises to adopt AI. This is recently evidenced by partnerships with OpenAI and Anthropic. The AI supplier landscape is increasingly fragmented, and corporate customers need significant help adopting these technologies at scale. We believe this will drive AI suppliers and customers into Accenture's arms. The current cyclical pressures are being attributed to structural issues, which we believe is incorrect, creating an attractive long-term set-up.

Smiths Group PLC (Smin Ln) (SMGZY) – International

Smiths is making tangible progress in its transition toward a more focused, higher-performing portfolio of industrial technology businesses. The Medical segment was divested in January 2022, with the exits of Interconnect and Detection expected to be complete in 2026. In parallel, management has simplified the operating structure and driven meaningful cost and capital efficiency improvements. The pro forma company (John Crane and Flex-Tek) is positioned for structurally higher growth, margins, and returns than the legacy conglomerate, underpinned by more concentrated exposure to flow-management and thermal-solutions markets, a high proportion of recurring revenue, and an active operational-excellence agenda. Their improved growth and profitability profile is complemented by a pristine balance sheet and substantial shareholder returns. While the shares have performed well recently, Smiths continues to trade at a modest valuation relative to its fundamental outlook and at a discount to its estimated break-up value.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AI: Short Circuit?
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Fiduciary Management Q4 2025 Investment Strategy Outlook.

AI: Short Circuit?

The impact AI has had on global stock markets and economic growth is simply staggering. JPMorgan (JPM) has identified 42 AI-related stocks in the S&P 500 (SP500), which today represent 45% of the index’s market cap. They estimate that these stocks have accounted for 78% of S&P 500 returns, 66% of earnings growth, and 71% of capital spending growth since ChatGPT launched in November 2022. Let that sink in...the narrow concentration over the last three years is astounding. Keeping up with the S&P 500 without leaning heavily into AI has been a virtual impossibility, per the table below. Excluding these 42 AI-related companies, the S&P 500 would have underperformed Europe, Japan, and China over the period.

Mega cap technology companies dominate the MSCI World Index (MSCI) as well, accounting for 9 of the top 10 holdings (all are U.S. companies). The High-Tech Strategist points out that “The U.S. accounts for just 4% of the world’s population and 24% of world GDP, yet these [top] 10 stocks account for 28.3% of the total MSCI World Index weighting. The total weighting of all U.S. stocks comes to 72.7% of the index. NVIDIA (NVDA) alone accounts for 6% of the index weight, more than all Japan’s stocks (5.5%). In country weights, Japan is second to the U.S.” This can be attributed to unwavering enthusiasm for just about anything that touches AI.

Returns, earnings and capex / R&D of AI-related stocks in the S&P 500 since ChatGPT launch in Q4 2022

Direct AI28 stocks AI Utilities8 stocks AI Cap Equip6 stocks Total AI42 stocks S&P 500ex-AI MSCIEurope MSCIJapan MSCIChina
Since November 2022
Price Return 195% 66% 174% 190% 26% 33% 59% 50%
Earnings growth 159% 64% 155% 153% 19% 4% 52% 15%
Capex / R&D growth 72% 13% 20% 68% 19% 28% 37% 27%

Share of changes since November 2022

Price Return 76% 0.8% 1.3% 78% 22%
Earnings growth 63% 1.6% 1.5% 66% 34%
Capex / R&D growth 70% 1.0% 0.2% 71% 29%
Source: Bloomberg, JPMAM, December 22, 2025

As it relates to the impact on the U.S. economy, JPM estimates tech sector capital spending contributed 40%-45% of U.S. GDP growth through the first 9 months of the year, up from less than 5% during the same period in 2023. As JPM depicts to the right, tech capex in 2025 dwarfs every major historical U.S. infrastructure project as a percentage of GDP. Well-known economist David Rosenberg pegs the AI impact on the economy as even greater, writing that “100% of the [1H25] GDP growth has come from the 8% chunk of the economy linked to the AI boom ... the other 92% is completely flat.” This is consistent with the weakness we are seeing elsewhere in the economy (i.e., employment, housing, manufacturing, construction, transportation). There are clearly cracks beneath the surface, with underlying economic growth much less robust than it appears.

Asset-Heavy Firms Underperform

As we wrote in our last letter, we see tremendous long-term potential for AI, but question whether the enormous amount of capital being spent will generate attractive returns and over what time frame. The numbers thrown around continue to mystify. OpenAI, for example, is looking to spend $1.4 trillion on AI infrastructure by 2033. The company, however, is only generating ~$20 billion in revenue, is unprofitable, burning cash, and completely dependent upon external financing. The math does not add up. OpenAI’s well-documented “circular” funding with its business partners (NVIDIA, Microsoft (MSFT), among others) is additional cause for concern.

Moreover, the top five hyperscalers are expected to spend over $500 billion on capex this year alone. The capital intensity of these businesses has sky-rocketed, with capex-to-revenue expected to reach 29% in aggregate in 2026. By way of comparison, well-known industrial company Caterpillar (CAT) has a historical capex-to-revenue of ~5%. One of the most capital-intensive industries in the world is railroads, where Union Pacific (UNP) has a comparable metric of around 14%. As illustrated below, capital intensity has not historically been well regarded by the stock market, with asset-light businesses increasingly outperforming over the last 30 years. Eventually, revenue will also need to follow. Bain Capital estimates AI companies will have to generate a whopping $2 trillion in combined revenue by 2030 to fund the computing power needed to meet expected demand, but are likely to fall short by around $800 billion. Something will have to give.

Tech capital spending in 2025 vs spending on major US infrastructure projects

Peak annual project percent of GDP

As we learned in the 2000 tech bubble, a ground-breaking technology (internet) can prove to be everything that was expected and more, but that may not be enough to mitigate the downside risks. Greenlight Capital’s David Einhorn opines, “Reflecting upon the internet bubble with the benefit of hindsight, whoever was the most bullish about the internet in the year 2000 was still not bullish enough. At the turn of the century, we had no smart phones, no tablets, no Wi-Fi, no Google, no Facebook, no TikTok, no cloud, and no video streaming. The internet has become much more important than even the biggest optimists believed.”

Importantly, that did not stop the Nasdaq (NASDAQ) index from falling 77% from its peak and 200 companies from going bankrupt.

If the AI stars do not carefully align, we could be in for a rough ride in the years ahead, with downside protection of paramount importance. In the meantime, we follow our DNA: buy strong companies that make money, avoid stressed balance sheets, stay disciplined on valuation, and keep it simple. While the market is less interested in our types of stories today, we view that as a historical anomaly. We will stay the course with confidence that better days lie ahead.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: J.B. Hunt 2026 Outlook - Improving Pricing, Volume Risks, Increased Expectations
data.description:
The last few years have been rough for transports in general, with excess capacity and often-weak demand hitting pricing while inflationary pressures hit margins. J.B. Hunt (JBHT) has held up better than many in some respects, with its strengths in areas like intermodal and dedicated trucking allowing the company to preserve EBTIDA margins in the 13%’s during this downturn while also generating free cash flow.

It’s been a while since I’ve written about J.B. Hunt for Seeking Alpha, and the shares have certainly shown some volatility over that time. Net-net, the low-20%’s gain in the share price is a bit below that of the broader transport space and well below the industrial sector, so I don’t feel too bad about a neutral stance then given my views on the longer-term return potential.

The call is a little more challenging now. I feel better about the outlook for JBHT going into 2026, but the share price move from the $130’s to over $200 since September 2025 captures a lot of that improved outlook. I can see some upside from here, but not enough to really get me all that excited about the shares, particularly with ongoing risks to demand in the wider economy.

The Guide Will Be Important

With stocks like JBHT, it often feels like the quarterly report only matters when it’s bad. If results are good but the guidance is soft, the stock will sell off. If the guidance is good but the results are soft, the stock will often sell off. It’s only when both are good that the Street really seems to get all that excited about the quarter that was.

In any case, I’m a little cautious regarding the upcoming results. It looks like this year’s peak season was pretty solid, so that should be good for the trucking businesses, especially with the truckload business having meaningful spot exposure. On the other hand, I’m not quite as bullish on intermodal given ongoing pricing pressures and weaker rail traffic, with intermodal down 3.4% in December, down 6.5% in November, and down 3% in October.

As far as margins go, I am concerned that the year-end surge in spot rates could create pressure in the brokerage business and I think Final Mile could likewise see pressure from softer volumes. On the positive side, management made rapid initial progress in the prior quarter with its $100M/yr cost reduction program, so there could be some incremental upside here to offset some of the business pressures.

I’m definitely curious as to what the guide for the first quarter will look like, and this will set an early tone for a range of peers like Hub Group (HUBG), Knight (KNX), RXO (RXO), Schneider (SNDR), and others. Fairly steep sequential declines from Q4 to Q1 are normal with this business, and with the Street currently looking for around a 5% step down, there could be some risk here if management sees ongoing softness in intermodal volumes.

Improving Rates, Mixed Demand Drivers

There have been more than a couple false dawns in the trucking and intermodal sectors over the past couple of years has capacity remained stubbornly high despite weak prices and rising costs. Looking out across 2026, pricing does seem to finally be firming up, but the overall outlook for demand is still mixed.

I’m incrementally more bullish on the trucking parts of JBHT’s business right now. With the current administration more aggressively enforcing regulations, drivers and trucks are leaving the market, leading to recent spot rates that are the highest in about three years. This supply correction has been long in coming, but there is a risk that idle capacity at trucking companies could be activated relatively quickly (provided they can find the drivers).

As far as trucking demand goes, things are more mixed. Housing remains weak and neither consumer nor business confidence are very strong right now. Destocking across many industrial end-markets should be positive for short-cycle demand and provide some support for trucking volumes, but cost inflation remains a risk.

I’m more concerned about intermodal, though I do think there will be some improvement as the year goes on. A still-weak manufacturing environment (an ISM PMI below 50) is a concern, but again I think low inventory levels will support some improvement in activity. Weaker transcon volumes on tariffs remain a threat, and comps will be more challenging as companies bought ahead of tariffs, and I do see a risk of overcapacity in the intermodal space without a stronger recovery in manufacturing activity to offset likely weaker import-related volumes.

Self-Help Can Be A Positive Driver

I might argue that JBHT doesn’t get enough credit for its long-term history of adapting to market changes, and I think that is still a relevant issue today.

Years ago the narrative on the stock was that management was making a mistake by hanging on to its dedicated and truckload trucking businesses and that intermodal and asset-light logistics is where the future was at. Now the Dedicated segment is more profitable than Intermodal and there are broader sector-wide concerns about excess capacity in intermodal and brokerage and trucking businesses have come into relatively greater favor.

Likewise, there are bearish talking points out there about JBHT’s vulnerability to the proposed Union Pacific (UNP) Norfolk Southern (NSC) merger. While Norfolk has been an important part of JBHT’s intermodal business, I don’t think a combined UP-NS will necessarily find it so easy to just switch away from JBHT (the idea being that because JBHT doesn’t do much business with UP, they’ll look to replace JBHT on those former NS volumes, perhaps with rivals like Hub Group). Operational disruptions are no trivial thing for rail operators and JBHT’s significant presence in the market would make it difficult at best to simply stop doing business with.

In terms of self-help, I think the bears may be underestimating JBHT’s ability to offset disruptions here, whether by doing more business with CSX (CSX) or other carriers or simply reallocating assets across its footprint.

I also see more self-help potential on the IT side of things. Relative to many of its rivals, JBHT’s scale gives it the ability to invest meaningful sums into IT on a relatively consistent basis. Although I think AI is more of buzzword in logistics today than a true driver, management has continued to invest in its systems and capabilities and I do think these investments will help drive more costs out of the system and better operating performance metrics.

The Outlook

I do expect 2026 to be better than 2025, but I’m starting off the year a little below the average sell-side revenue target (about 2% below) given my ongoing concerns about persistent soft volumes. I am fairly bullish on pricing though, and management has started to finally see some pricing improvements in intermodal contracts, and I think business should accelerate through the year.

Looking beyond 2026 I’m more bullish on growth, and I’m expecting around 5% annualized revenue growth and a long-term annualized growth rate between 4% and 5%.

On the margin side, I expect a meaningful improvement in 2026 through better asset utilization, improving volumes, and the realization of those cost reduction efforts. I’m expecting EBITDA margin to improve to around 14%, with upside to around 14.5% if business develops better than I expect. Going out a few more years I think 15% margins are possible. Those margin improvements should drive free cash flow margins back into the 6% range, supporting around 7% annualized FCF growth.

Valuation is challenging, but that’s not exactly a new development with J.B. Hunt, as the shares have long traded at robust multiples (including low-to-mid-teen EBITDA multiples and P/E multiples in the low-20’s). A 13x multiple on forward EBITDA gives a little upside from here (to around $218), and I don’t rule out the possibility of beat-and-raise quarters if the economy strengthens more than I expect in 2026.

I do also want to note that JBHT management has been good about returning capital to shareholders. Unlike some companies that advertise buybacks but don’t really deliver, or simply use buybacks to offset dilution from executive stock awards, JBHT has reduced its share count by around 40% over the past two decades, and the company announced another $1B buyback authorization back in October that should reduce the share count by another 4%-6%.

The Bottom Line

J.B. Hunt is another good example of a situation where I like the company but don’t often love the stock given an often-elevated valuation. With a 10-year return of about 12.5% versus a 15.5% return for the S&P 500, a 14.5% return for the broader industrial sector, and an 11% return for the transport sector, I don’t feel like I’ve really missed out by not paying up for the shares.

Should JBHT sell off again, whether on disappointing results/guidance for Q4/Q1 or a broader sell-off in the sector, it’s a name I’d revisit. But given a still-challenging end-market outlook and a pretty robust rebound in the valuation and Street expectations, I’m not inclined to chase it now.

data.title: Fiduciary Management Q4 2025 Investment Strategy Outlook
data.description:
The intoxication with Artificial Intelligence (AI) related companies continued in 2025, in conjunction with a low-quality “junk” rally that added fuel to the fire. Investors who were willing to extend out along the risk curve were rewarded. The Russell 2000 (RUT), S&P 500 (SP500), Msci Eafe (Local/USD), and MSCI World (MSCI WORLD) gained 12.81%, 17.88%, 20.60%/31.22%, and 21.09%, respectively. Many Wall Street strategists are now penciling in greater than 10% returns for the S&P 500 again in 2026, which would mark four consecutive years of double-digit returns for only the third time in history. It last occurred in the lead-up to the dot-com crash in the late 1990s, with an earlier iteration in the post-war period (1949-52). In the current cycle, record U.S. valuations have failed to put a damper on speculative behavior. Quite the opposite, in fact.

Each of FMI’s portfolios have generated solid double-digit annual returns over the past 3 years, but as expected, have not kept pace with the broader indices given the backdrop. While FMI’s discipline on business quality, balance sheet strength, and valuation have served our clients well over our 45+ year history, these investment attributes have been shunned in the current climate. We underwrite calculated risks and remain true to our process. As the stock market exhibits increasing signs of a potential bubble, we continue to proceed with restraint and prudence.

Junkyard Rally

Equity market index providers such as S&P, Russell, and MSCI each define “quality” businesses a bit differently. A variety of factors are used for classification, including return on equity (ROE), profitability, balance sheet leverage, earnings variability, and cash flow (i.e., accruals ratio). These are generally consistent with how FMI defines quality: businesses with sustainable competitive advantages and barriers to entry, return on invested capital (ROIC) above cost of capital, strong earnings quality (cash flow generation) and predictability, and a robust balance sheet. As illustrated in the chart on the top, high-quality has underperformed low-quality sharply in the U.S. in 2025 (per the downward sloping blue line), despite outperforming over the long run.

Low-quality’s recent outperformance has been especially acute in the small cap universe, where companies that lose money, have a low ROE, do not have sales, or are high beta have dominated since the market bottomed on April 8, 2025. The Jefferies graphic on the bottom illustrates how each subset has performed versus the overall benchmark (Russell 2000, or RUT), which is eye opening. As depicted, small cap active managers have struggled to keep pace during this junk rally.

Overseas, quality has been a meaningful laggard as well, with the Msci Eafe (EAFE) Quality Index underperforming the Msci Eafe by ~10% in both local FX and USD. According to Goldman Sachs, 2025’s top performing themes in Europe were Defense (+89.9%), Rate Sensitive Financials (+72.1%), and EU Miners (+55.5%). FMI considers most of the constituents in these buckets to be at the low-end of the quality spectrum and thus has limited exposure.

Leuthold 3000 Universe is composed of the largest 3,000 securities traded on the U.S. exchanges. November 2025.

Big rally by lower-quality groups; really hurting active managers

For those with a value orientation, as we deploy at FMI, a tilt toward quality has worked quite well over the long-term. The Leuthold Group tracks “Quality Value” (cheap stocks that also rank high on quality metrics) versus other gradients of value: “Value” (cheap stocks) and “Junky Value” (cheap stocks that rank low on quality metrics). Despite the recent headwinds, Quality Value’s long-term relative outperformance is unmistakable, as illustrated below.

So why does Quality Value work?

  • Good businesses with sound balance sheets are better equipped to navigate difficult times.
  • Earning a ROIC above cost of capital creates economic value; reinvesting at high incremental returns builds a powerful compounding effect.
  • Investing in businesses with organic growth helps to avoid value traps (i.e. secular decline).
  • Buying out-of-favor businesses at a discount to intrinsic value creates a margin of safety.
  • Strong balance sheets allow for self-financing, avoiding expensive debt and equity issuance.
  • Weak balance sheets can lead to insolvency or bankruptcy in distressed situations.

Given all of the above factors, Quality Value tends to offer superior downside protection during market downturns. Ultimately, buying advantaged businesses at discount valuations is a winning formula. Rallies like we are witnessing today are typically short-lived.

AI: Short Circuit?

The impact AI has had on global stock markets and economic growth is simply staggering. JPMorgan (JPM) has identified 42 AI-related stocks in the S&P 500, which today represent 45% of the index’s market cap. They estimate that these stocks have accounted for 78% of S&P 500 returns, 66% of earnings growth, and 71% of capital spending growth since ChatGPT launched in November 2022. Let that sink in...the narrow concentration over the last three years is astounding. Keeping up with the S&P 500 without leaning heavily into AI has been a virtual impossibility, per the table below. Excluding these 42 AI-related companies, the S&P 500 would have underperformed Europe, Japan, and China over the period.

Mega cap technology companies dominate the MSCI World Index as well, accounting for 9 of the top 10 holdings (all are U.S. companies). The High-Tech Strategist points out that “The U.S. accounts for just 4% of the world’s population and 24% of world GDP, yet these [top] 10 stocks account for 28.3% of the total MSCI World Index weighting. The total weighting of all U.S. stocks comes to 72.7% of the index. NVIDIA (NVDA) alone accounts for 6% of the index weight, more than all Japan’s stocks (5.5%). In country weights, Japan is second to the U.S.” This can be attributed to unwavering enthusiasm for just about anything that touches AI.
